preFix.title: Whittled sycamore butter curler
preFix.subtitle: A ridged sycamore tool for producing butter curls
preFix.excerpt: A butter curler is a small tool with a ridged working edge that is drawn across cold butter to produce decorative curls. Sycamore is food-safe and resists the cold moisture of the butter block.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the small-treen kitchen-tool form; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the butter-curler implement and its use at table.

content.text: A butter curler is a small flat tool with a hooked or ridged working edge. It is drawn across a block of cold butter in a single stroke to lift a curl. The version in this tutorial has a grooved flat edge: two parallel ridges cut into the working face, which produces a fluted curl rather than a plain one. Sycamore is the recommendation because it is food-safe, does not flavour the butter, and
seasoned
sycamore resists the light cold-moisture environment of butter use better than softer species.
Wood
Sycamore is the recommendation: food-safe, close-grained, and hard enough to hold the ridges without chipping. The
blank

needs to be 100 mm long, 18 mm wide, and 8 mm thick. Cut it from a piece of dried sycamore board or a flat slab. Beech is the alternative: similarly food-safe and even harder, which makes the ridges more durable.
Tools
Sloyd knife: for the outline, handle shaping, and the ridge cuts.
Leather strop: strop every fifteen minutes.
Raw linseed oil: food-safe finish. Two-week cure before food contact.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
Shaping the body

The butter curler is 100 mm long. The working end is 18 mm wide and 6 mm thick; the handle tapers to 12 mm wide and 7 mm thick over the 40 mm of the grip section. Draw the outline on the face of the blank. Use push cuts along the sides to bring the profile to the line. The handle is slightly thicker than the working end to give leverage.
The working edge
The working face is the face of the blank at the wide end. Cut two ridges along the working face using
chip cuts

. Each ridge is a V-channel 1.5 mm wide and 1 mm deep, running the full length of the working end (60 mm) and parallel to the long axis. The two channels are 4 mm apart. The ridges between the channels and at each side are the parts that contact the butter.

To cut a V-channel: make one angled cut from the left at 45 degrees to the surface, running the length of the channel. Set the cut at 1 mm deep. Then make a second cut from the right at 45 degrees meeting the first cut at the bottom. The chip between the two cuts lifts cleanly from sycamore.
Finishing
Apply raw linseed oil to all surfaces. Let soak for one hour, wipe off surplus. Repeat after 24 hours. Allow two-week cure before first use on butter.
Using the curler

Chill the curler in cold water for five minutes before use. Draw the ridged face across a cold block of butter (straight from the fridge) in a single smooth stroke with light pressure. The butter curls over the ridges and peels away in a fluted coil. Rinse the curler in cold water between curls.
Variations
Three ridges: add a third V-channel for a wider curl.
Hooked working end: carve a gentle 10 degree hook onto the working end so the curl catches under the hook rather than flying off.
Beech version: harder grain takes sharper ridge edges and produces a crisper curl.
Care
Rinse in cold water immediately after use; hot water melts the butter into the grain. Never dishwasher. Dry and re-oil monthly.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Keep V-channels to 1 mm depth maximum. If a ridge broke off, re-cut the working section to remove the break and start the channels again from fresh wood.
attrs.cause: Cuts made too deep or across the grain

attrs.fix: Chill the curler in cold water for at least five minutes. The ridge surfaces must be smooth; light sanding of the ridge faces with 320-grit improves release.
attrs.cause: Curler not chilled before use or surface too rough
attrs.symptom: The butter sticks to the curler rather than releasing

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
